revision.text:
{{特殊文字|説明=JIS X 0212（[[補助漢字]]）に収録}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''多利思北孤'''（たりしひこ）は、『[[隋書]]』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}」で記述される[[倭国]]王である。『隋書』では他の中国史書が「[[倭]]」としている文字を「{{補助漢字フォント|俀}}」と記述している<ref>日本では通常、「{{補助漢字フォント|俀}}國」は「倭國」の誤りとされる。</ref>。『[[北史]]』にも記されている。
== 概要 == [[開皇]]20年（[[600年]]）と[[大業]]3年（[[607年]]）に[[隋]]に使者（[[遣隋使]]）を送ったという。
=== 名 === 「{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}」とあり、[[姓]]は阿毎、[[字]]は多利思北孤、[[号 (称号)|号]]は阿輩雞彌という<ref>[[大王 (ヤマト王権)]]参照</ref>。
=== 妻子 === 「{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}」とあり、妻は雞彌、後宮に600-700人の女がおり、太子の名は利歌彌多弗利という。
=== 領地 === 「{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}」とあり、里数を知らず日で距離を測る。国境は東西を旅するのに五ヶ月、南北を旅するのに三ヶ月かかり、それぞれ海に行き着く。「邪靡堆」を都としており[[魏志倭人伝|魏志]]の[[邪馬台国|邪馬臺]]であるとする。
「{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}」とあり、[[阿蘇山]]があり理由なく火を噴き天に接し、祷祭する。
[[隋]]使の[[裴世清]]らの道程は「{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}」とあり、大海の都斯麻国（対馬）、東に[[一支国]]、[[竹斯国]]（筑紫）、東に[[秦王国]]他10余国をへて海岸についたという。竹斯国から東はすべて俀であるという。
=== 政治 === 「{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}」とあり、天を兄とし、日を弟とした。天が明けぬうち出てあぐらをかいて座り政務し、日が出ると政務をやめ弟にゆだねた。隋の[[楊堅|高祖]]は義理がないとしてこれを改めさせたという。
また、「{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}」とあり、12の官（[[冠位十二階]]）制度があるという<ref>『[[日本書紀]]』、『[[上宮聖徳法王帝説]]』の記述とは順序が異なっている。</ref>。
=== 日出處天子 === 大業3年（607年）の国書に「{{lang|zh|聞海西菩薩天子重興佛法故遣朝拜兼沙門數十人來學佛法 其國書曰 日出處天子致書日沒處天子無恙云云}}」とあり、仏教を学ぶための使者の国書が有名な「日出處天子致書日沒處天子無恙云云」であり、開皇11年（591年）菩薩戒により総持菩薩となった文帝（[[楊堅]]）の子、[[煬帝]]を怒らせた（「{{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}」）。文帝の死、煬帝の即位を倭は知らなかった。「日出處」「日沒處」は当時の仏典（『摩訶般若波羅蜜多経』の注釈書『大智度論』など）に「日出処是東方 日没処是西方」とあり東西の方角を表す表現でもある。
ここで、[[隋書]]には過去の倭が授かっていた、朝鮮半島の将軍や倭国王への任命や称号を日本側から求めたり、[[隋]]も印綬を多利思比孤へ支給する記述が出現しない<ref>吉村武彦 『古代天皇の誕生』p.110 角川書店 ISBN 978-4047032972</ref>。このことから単なる方角を表す表現と断定はできない。
煬帝は怒ったが、[[高句麗]]の背後にある倭を重視し、[[裴世清]]を倭に派遣した。『[[日本書紀]]』は裴世清が持参した返書を載せており、「皇帝問倭皇」から始まっている。倭皇は「倭王」を改竄したものとする論者が多いが、煬帝から「皇」を与えられたと改竄しても[[皇国史観]]の補強にはならず、半ば皮肉を込めて倭王の無礼を受け容れたとも考えられる。『日本書紀』では日本側はこの「皇」を取り入れて「[[天皇]]」を名乗ったように書かれている。
== 解釈 == 『隋書』や『北史』はこの王を妻のいる男性としており、この時期に男性の大王は『[[日本書紀]]』、『[[古事記]]』には登場しない。『[[旧唐書]]』卷199上 列傳第149上 東夷 倭國 においても倭国の王の姓は阿毎氏であるとしている。『[[新唐書]]』卷220列傳第145 東夷 日本に「{{lang|zh|用明 亦曰目多利思比孤直隋開皇末 始與中國通}}」とあり多利思北孤を多利思比孤とし[[用明天皇]]としている。
日本では[[直木孝次郎]]による多利思'''北'''孤は多利思'''比'''孤の誤りとする説が通説となっている。また、[[推古天皇]]か厩戸皇子（[[聖徳太子]]・用明天皇の嫡子）のことだとする論者もいる。また、多利思比狐の「比狐」（ひこ）は男性と思われるので、推古天皇ではなく当時の有力者である蘇我馬子か聖徳太子であるという説もある。600年の使者も607年と同じ[[小野妹子]]であったと推定し、その先祖とされる[[孝昭天皇]]の皇子・[[天足彦国押人命]]の名前とする説もある<ref name="Ishihara"/><ref>天足彦国押人命は娘の[[押媛]]が弟の[[孝安天皇]]の后となり甥で孫の[[孝霊天皇]]を産んだとされ、女系では以後の皇室の先祖ともされる。</ref>。『[[釈日本紀]]』に引かれた『[[筑前国]][[風土記]]』逸文では[[アメノヒボコ]]を「[[高句麗|高麗]]国の意呂山に天より降り来たりし日桙」と記していることも留意される。
太子名（固有名詞説と普通名詞説がある）のうち利を和の誤りとする説がある。古来の[[大和言葉]]では、原則として「ら行」音は語頭に立たない（[[万葉仮名]]では語頭にr音が来ない）ことから、「利」を「和」の誤りとして「利歌彌多弗利」を「和歌彌多弗利」とする。また、「和歌彌多弗利」を[[源氏物語]]等にもあらわれる「わかんどほり（皇室の血統、皇族）」とする説もある<ref name="Ishihara">石原道博 (編訳)『新訂 魏志倭人伝・後漢書倭伝・宋書倭国伝・隋書倭国伝 中国正史日本伝 (1)』 （岩波書店、1985年）</ref>。なお、『[[翰苑]]』には「王長子号和哥彌多弗利。華言太子。」とある。
中国風に姓と字に分割されているが、タラ（リ）シヒコは人名ではなく、日本語の意味を理解していなかった中国人が誤解したものという説がある<!-- 参考･後述書 『大王から天皇へ』 p.236.『王権をめぐる戦い』 p.53.「天垂らし彦」で倭王の一つの称号。 --><ref>[[森公章]] 『日本の時代史3 倭国から日本へ』 [[吉川弘文館]] 2002年 ISBN 4-642-00803-9 p.8.</ref><ref>編集[[白石太一郎]] [[吉村武彦]] 『新視点 日本の歴史2 古代編Ⅰ 古代►飛鳥時代』 [[新人物往来社]] 1993年 ISBN 4-404-02002-3 pp.313 - 314.</ref>。文献では、姓はアメ、字はタラシヒコと記述されている<ref>[[吉田孝]]は[[岩波新書]]『日本の誕生』で「中国の役人は、倭王にも当然、姓はあるはずと考えていたので（中略）問い詰められた倭の使者が、苦しまぎれに「姓はアメ」と答えたのかもしれない」と推測している。</ref>が、日本語では、「天垂らし彦」になり、天から垂れた（降りた）男子という意であり<ref>[[熊谷公男]] 『日本の歴史03 大王から天皇へ』 [[講談社]] 2001年 ISBN 4-06-268903-0 p.237.</ref><ref>[[岸俊男]] 『日本の古代6 王権をめぐる戦い』 [[中央公論社]] 1986年 ISBN 4-12-402539-4 p.53.</ref>、つまり「天孫」という意味になる。中国語では「[[天子]]」（『[[通典]]』では「天児」）がこれに当たるが、中国の天子とは意味が異なる<ref>『新視点 日本の歴史2 古代編Ⅰ』 p.314.</ref>。一方で、[[熊谷公男]]は『[[万葉集]]』の「天の原 振り放（さ）けみれば 大王の 御寿（みいのち）は長く '''天足らしたり'''」（巻二から一四七）の歌などを参考に、「天の満ち足りた男子」という意味の尊称と解釈している<ref>同『日本の歴史03 大王から天皇へ』 p.237.</ref>（この説は[[森田悌]]も支持している<ref>[[森田悌]] 『推古朝と聖徳太子』 [[岩田書院]] 2005年 ISBN 4-87294-391-0 p.145.</ref>）。森田悌は[[邪馬台国]]の時代では、「天垂らし彦」の称号があったとは考えがたいとし<ref>同『推古朝と聖徳太子』 p.145.</ref>、以後の時代に大陸思想の影響から芽生えたとみている<ref>同『推古朝と聖徳太子』 p.146.</ref>（また、「天子」という語が反感を受けたのに対し、「天垂らし彦」の反応が低かったことに注目している<!-- 同『推古朝と聖徳太子』 -->）。[[王仲殊]]も阿毎多利思比孤は「天足彦（天の満ち足りた男子）」とした（天垂らし彦説もあると紹介した）上で<!-- 後述書 pp.133 - 134. -->、この語の中にはすでに「天子」「天皇」といった意味が含まれており、これは最初の国書で日中両国の君主を共に「天子」と称したため、中国側の不快感をあおったところから、それぞれ天子を「皇帝」と「天皇」と呼び変えて区別を示したとする<ref>[[王仲殊]] [[西嶋定生]]監訳 [[桐本東太]]訳 『中国からみた古代日本』 [[学生社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-311-20181-8 p.145.</ref>。
倭王が天や日と兄弟であるという説明は記紀神話とは一致しないが、兄弟の順序は[[神獣鏡]]にしばしば刻まれる「天王日月」と一致する。『[[日本書紀]]』神代上の本文では[[イザナギ|伊弉諾尊]]と[[イザナミ|伊弉冉尊]]が「いかにぞ天下の主者を生まざらむ」と言って日神と月神の兄弟（姉弟）を産んだとされ、一書では伊弉諾尊が白[[銅鏡]]を持って日と月を産んだとされる。熊谷は[[高句麗]]の王権思想の借用かとしている<ref>熊谷公男『日本の歴史03 大王から天皇へ』 p.235.</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{特殊文字|説明=JIS X 0212（[[補助漢字]]）に収録}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''多利思北孤'''（たりしひこ）は、『[[隋書]]』「{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}」で記述される[[倭国]]王である。『隋書』では他の中国史書が「[[倭]]」としている文字を「{{補助漢字フォント|俀}}」と記述している<ref>日本では通常、「{{補助漢字フォント|俀}}國」は「倭國」の誤りとされる。</ref>。『[[北史]]』にも記されている。
== 概要 == [[開皇]]20年（[[600年]]）と[[大業]]3年（[[607年]]）に[[隋]]に使者（[[遣隋使]]）を送ったという。
=== 名 === 「{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}」とあり、[[姓]]は阿毎、[[字]]は多利思北孤、[[号 (称号)|号]]は阿輩雞彌という<ref>[[大王 (ヤマト王権)]]参照</ref>。
=== 妻子 === 「{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}」とあり、妻は雞彌、後宮に600-700人の女がおり、太子の名は利歌彌多弗利という。
=== 領地 === 「{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}」とあり、里数を知らず日で距離を測る。国境は東西を旅するのに五ヶ月、南北を旅するのに三ヶ月かかり、それぞれ海に行き着く。「邪靡堆」を都としており[[魏志倭人伝|魏志]]の[[邪馬台国|邪馬臺]]であるとする。
「{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}」とあり、[[阿蘇山]]があり理由なく火を噴き天に接し、祷祭する。
[[隋]]使の[[裴世清]]らの道程は「{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}」とあり、大海の都斯麻国（対馬）、東に[[一支国]]、[[竹斯国]]（筑紫）、東に[[秦王国]]他10余国をへて海岸についたという。竹斯国から東はすべて俀であるという。
=== 政治 === 「{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}」とあり、天を兄とし、日を弟とした。天が明けぬうち出てあぐらをかいて座り政務し、日が出ると政務をやめ弟にゆだねた。隋の[[楊堅|高祖]]は義理がないとしてこれを改めさせたという。
また、「{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}」とあり、12の官（[[冠位十二階]]）制度があるという<ref>『[[日本書紀]]』、『[[上宮聖徳法王帝説]]』の記述とは順序が異なっている。</ref>。
=== 日出處天子 === 大業3年（607年）の国書に「{{lang|zh|聞海西菩薩天子重興佛法故遣朝拜兼沙門數十人來學佛法 其國書曰 日出處天子致書日沒處天子無恙云云}}」とあり、仏教を学ぶための使者の国書が有名な「日出處天子致書日沒處天子無恙云云」であり、開皇11年（591年）菩薩戒により総持菩薩となった文帝（[[楊堅]]）の子、[[煬帝]]を怒らせた（「{{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}」）。文帝の死、煬帝の即位を倭は知らなかった。「日出處」「日沒處」は当時の仏典（『摩訶般若波羅蜜多経』の注釈書『大智度論』など）に「日出処是東方 日没処是西方」とあり東西の方角を表す表現でもある。
ここで、[[隋書]]には過去の倭が授かっていた、朝鮮半島の将軍や倭国王への任命や称号を日本側から求めたり、[[隋]]も印綬を多利思比孤へ支給する記述が出現しない<ref>吉村武彦 『古代天皇の誕生』p.110 角川書店 ISBN 978-4047032972</ref>。このことから単なる方角を表す表現と断定はできない。
煬帝は怒ったが、[[高句麗]]の背後にある倭を重視し、[[裴世清]]を倭に派遣した。『[[日本書紀]]』は裴世清が持参した返書を載せており、「皇帝問倭皇」から始まっている。倭皇は「倭王」を改竄したものとする論者が多いが、煬帝から「皇」を与えられたと改竄しても[[皇国史観]]の補強にはならず、半ば皮肉を込めて倭王の無礼を受け容れたとも考えられる。『日本書紀』では日本側はこの「皇」を取り入れて「[[天皇]]」を名乗ったように書かれている。
== 解釈 == 『隋書』や『北史』はこの王を妻のいる男性としており、この時期に男性の大王は『[[日本書紀]]』、『[[古事記]]』には登場しない。『[[旧唐書]]』卷199上 列傳第149上 東夷 倭國 においても倭国の王の姓は阿毎氏であるとしている。『[[新唐書]]』卷220列傳第145 東夷 日本に「{{lang|zh|用明 亦曰目多利思比孤直隋開皇末 始與中國通}}」とあり多利思北孤を多利思比孤とし[[用明天皇]]としている。
日本では[[直木孝次郎]]による多利思'''北'''孤は多利思'''比'''孤の誤りとする説が通説となっている。また、[[推古天皇]]か厩戸皇子（[[聖徳太子]]・用明天皇の嫡子）のことだとする論者もいる。また、多利思比狐の「比狐」（ひこ）は男性と思われるので、推古天皇ではなく当時の有力者である蘇我馬子か聖徳太子であるという説もある。600年の使者も607年と同じ[[小野妹子]]であったと推定し、その先祖とされる[[孝昭天皇]]の皇子・[[天足彦国押人命]]の名前とする説もある<ref name="Ishihara"/><ref>天足彦国押人命は娘の[[押媛]]が弟の[[孝安天皇]]の后となり甥で孫の[[孝霊天皇]]を産んだとされ、女系では以後の皇室の先祖ともされる。</ref>。『[[釈日本紀]]』に引かれた『[[筑前国]][[風土記]]』逸文では[[アメノヒボコ]]を「[[高句麗|高麗]]国の意呂山に天より降り来たりし日桙」と記していることも留意される。
太子名（固有名詞説と普通名詞説がある）のうち利を和の誤りとする説がある。古来の[[大和言葉]]では、原則として「ら行」音は語頭に立たない（[[万葉仮名]]では語頭にr音が来ない）ことから、「利」を「和」の誤りとして「利歌彌多弗利」を「和歌彌多弗利」とする。また、「和歌彌多弗利」を[[源氏物語]]等にもあらわれる「わかんどほり（皇室の血統、皇族）」とする説もある<ref name="Ishihara">石原道博 (編訳)『新訂 魏志倭人伝・後漢書倭伝・宋書倭国伝・隋書倭国伝 中国正史日本伝 (1)』 （岩波書店、1985年）</ref>。なお、『[[翰苑]]』には「王長子号和哥彌多弗利。華言太子。」とある。
中国風に姓と字に分割されているが、タラ（リ）シヒコは人名ではなく、日本語の意味を理解していなかった中国人が誤解したものという説がある<!-- 参考･後述書 『大王から天皇へ』 p.236.『王権をめぐる戦い』 p.53.「天垂らし彦」で倭王の一つの称号。 --><ref>[[森公章]] 『日本の時代史3 倭国から日本へ』 [[吉川弘文館]] 2002年 ISBN 4-642-00803-9 p.8.</ref><ref>編集[[白石太一郎]] [[吉村武彦]] 『新視点 日本の歴史2 古代編Ⅰ 古代►飛鳥時代』 [[新人物往来社]] 1993年 ISBN 4-404-02002-3 pp.313 - 314.</ref>。文献では、姓はアメ、字はタラシヒコと記述されている<ref>[[吉田孝]]は[[岩波新書]]『日本の誕生』で「中国の役人は、倭王にも当然、姓はあるはずと考えていたので（中略）問い詰められた倭の使者が、苦しまぎれに「姓はアメ」と答えたのかもしれない」と推測している。</ref>が、日本語では、「天垂らし彦」になり、天から垂れた（降りた）男子という意であり<ref>[[熊谷公男]] 『日本の歴史03 大王から天皇へ』 [[講談社]] 2001年 ISBN 4-06-268903-0 p.237.</ref><ref>[[岸俊男]] 『日本の古代6 王権をめぐる戦い』 [[中央公論社]] 1986年 ISBN 4-12-402539-4 p.53.</ref>、つまり「天孫」という意味になる。中国語では「[[天子]]」（『[[通典]]』では「天児」）がこれに当たるが、中国の天子とは意味が異なる<ref>『新視点 日本の歴史2 古代編Ⅰ』 p.314.</ref>。一方で、[[熊谷公男]]は『[[万葉集]]』の「天の原 振り放（さ）けみれば 大王の 御寿（みいのち）は長く '''天足らしたり'''」（巻二から一四七）の歌などを参考に、「天の満ち足りた男子」という意味の尊称と解釈している<ref>同『日本の歴史03 大王から天皇へ』 p.237.</ref>（この説は[[森田悌]]も支持している<ref>[[森田悌]] 『推古朝と聖徳太子』 [[岩田書院]] 2005年 ISBN 4-87294-391-0 p.145.</ref>）。森田悌は[[邪馬台国]]の時代では、「天垂らし彦」の称号があったとは考えがたいとし<ref>同『推古朝と聖徳太子』 p.145.</ref>、以後の時代に大陸思想の影響から芽生えたとみている<ref>同『推古朝と聖徳太子』 p.146.</ref>（また、「天子」という語が反感を受けたのに対し、「天垂らし彦」の反応が低かったことに注目している<!-- 同『推古朝と聖徳太子』 -->）。[[王仲殊]]も阿毎多利思比孤は「天足彦（天の満ち足りた男子）」とした（天垂らし彦説もあると紹介した）上で<!-- 後述書 pp.133 - 134. -->、この語の中にはすでに「天子」「天皇」といった意味が含まれており、これは最初の国書で日中両国の君主を共に「天子」と称したため、中国側の不快感をあおったところから、それぞれ天子を「皇帝」と「天皇」と呼び変えて区別を示したとする<ref>[[王仲殊]] [[西嶋定生]]監訳 [[桐本東太]]訳 『中国からみた古代日本』 [[学生社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-311-20181-8 p.145.</ref>。
倭王が天や日と兄弟であるという説明は記紀神話とは一致しないが、兄弟の順序は[[神獣鏡]]にしばしば刻まれる「天王日月」と一致する。『[[日本書紀]]』神代上の本文では[[イザナギ|伊弉諾尊]]と[[イザナミ|伊弉冉尊]]が「いかにぞ天下の主者を生まざらむ」と言って日神と月神の兄弟（姉弟）を産んだとされ、一書では伊弉諾尊が白[[銅鏡]]を持って日と月を産んだとされる。熊谷は[[高句麗]]の王権思想の借用かとしている<ref>熊谷公男『日本の歴史03 大王から天皇へ』 p.235.</ref>。

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tarishihiko]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tarishihiko|Tarishihiko]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}
{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = King of Wa (possibly one of the Five kings of Wa) }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Accounts of Wa
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tarishihiko]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tarishihiko|Tarishihiko]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}
{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = King of Wa (possibly one of the Five kings of Wa) }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{reflist}}

In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.

=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>

The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. {{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]

{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>

The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").

Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10932442]] Wikidata name: Tarishihiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tarishihiko]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tarishihiko|Tarishihiko]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}
{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = King of Wa (possibly one of the Five kings of Wa) }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}
{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = {{ill|Ōkimi|qid=Q718995}} | gender= {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424}} }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=|1=Sokpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}
{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = {{ill|Ōkimi|qid=Q718995}} | gender= {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424}} }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|qq=|1=Sắc phong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]

{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref>
{{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}}
{{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}}
'''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.

== Overview ==
It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).

=== Name ===
The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>

=== Wife and Children ===
The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").

=== Territory ===
The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")

The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").

=== Governance ===
The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").

Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>

=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" ===
In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.

Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

== Interpretation ==
The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''. 

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref>

{{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}}
'''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of [[:en:Wakoku|Wa]] as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''[[:en:History of the Northern Dynasties|Book of the Northern Dynasties]]''.

== Overview ==
It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ([[:en:Japanese missions to Sui China|Mission to Sui]]) to [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).

=== Name ===
The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See [[:en:Okimi|Great King (Yamato court)]].</ref>

=== Wife and Children ===
The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").

=== Territory ===
The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")

The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").

=== Governance ===
The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").

Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>

=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" ===
In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.

Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

== Interpretation ==
The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''. 

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref>

The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")

The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").

=== Governance ===
The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").

Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>

=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" ===
In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.

Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

== Interpretation ==
The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''. 

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref>
