=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ([[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]]).<ref>The order differs from that in ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[:en:Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu]]''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Sắc phong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] * [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] * [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}

{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox person
| name               = Tarashiko
| birth_date         = Unknown
| birth_place        = Kingdom of Wa
| death_date         = Unkown
| death_place        = Unkown
| title              = {{ill|Ōkimi|WD=Q718995}}
| gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}
| nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}
}}

{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref>
{{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}}
{{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}}
'''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wa}} as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''{{ill|History of the Northern Dynasties|en|History of the Northern Dynasties|WD=Q1188548|lt=Book of the Northern Dynasties}}''.

== Overview ==
It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ({{ill|Japanese missions to Sui China|en|Japanese missions to Sui China|WD=Q15401774|lt=Mission to Sui}}) to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui}} in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).

=== Name ===
The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Great King (Yamato court)}}.</ref>

=== Wife and Children ===
The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").

=== Territory ===
The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")

The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").

=== Governance ===
The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").

Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>

=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" ===
In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.

Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

== Interpretation ==
The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''. 

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref>

Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.

Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

== Interpretation ==
The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''. 

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref>

{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox person
| name               = Tarashiko
| birth_date         = Unknown
| birth_place        = Kingdom of Wa
| death_date         = Unkown
| death_place        = Unkown
| title              = {{ill|Ōkimi|WD=Q718995}}
| gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}
| nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}
}}

{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref>
{{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}}
{{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}}
'''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wa}} as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''{{ill|History of the Northern Dynasties|en|History of the Northern Dynasties|WD=Q1188548|lt=Book of the Northern Dynasties}}''.

== Overview ==
It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ({{ill|Japanese missions to Sui China|en|Japanese missions to Sui China|WD=Q15401774|lt=Mission to Sui}}) to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui}} in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).

=== Name ===
The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Great King (Yamato court)}}.</ref>

=== Wife and Children ===
The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").

=== Territory ===
The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")

The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").

=== Governance ===
The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").

Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>

=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" ===
In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.

Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

== Interpretation ==
The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''. 

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref>

== Interpretation ==
The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''. 

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多利思比孤]][[zh:多利思比孤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多利思比孤]][[zh:多利思比孤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多利思比孤]][[zh:多利思比孤]]
{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}
{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = {{ill|Ōkimi|WD=Q718995}} | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}} }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wa}} as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''{{ill|History of the Northern Dynasties|en|History of the Northern Dynasties|WD=Q1188548|lt=Book of the Northern Dynasties}}''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ({{ill|Japanese missions to Sui China|en|Japanese missions to Sui China|WD=Q15401774|lt=Mission to Sui}}) to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui}} in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Great King (Yamato court)}}.</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Sắc phong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} * {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei}} * {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]

{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wa}} as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''{{ill|History of the Northern Dynasties|en|History of the Northern Dynasties|WD=Q1188548|lt=Book of the Northern Dynasties}}''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ({{ill|Japanese missions to Sui China|en|Japanese missions to Sui China|WD=Q15401774|lt=Mission to Sui}}) to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui}} in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Great King (Yamato court)}}.</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").

The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").

Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>

The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Sắc phong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} * {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei}} * {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = {{ill|Ōkimi|WD=Q718995|1=Okimi}} | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|1=Wakoku}} }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wa|1=Wakoku}} as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''{{ill|History of the Northern Dynasties|en|History of the Northern Dynasties|WD=Q1188548|lt=Book of the Northern Dynasties|1=History of the Northern Dynasties}}''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ({{ill|Japanese missions to Sui China|en|Japanese missions to Sui China|WD=Q15401774|lt=Mission to Sui|1=Japanese missions to Sui China}}) to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui|1=Sui dynasty}} in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Great King (Yamato court)|1=Okimi}}.</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|1=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Q29583251|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} * {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} * {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]

Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|1=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>

The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|1=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Q29583251|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} * {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} * {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = {{ill|Ōkimi|WD=Q718995|1=Okimi}} | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|1=Wakoku}} }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wa|1=Wakoku}} as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''{{ill|History of the Northern Dynasties|en|History of the Northern Dynasties|WD=Q1188548|lt=Book of the Northern Dynasties|1=History of the Northern Dynasties}}''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ({{ill|Japanese missions to Sui China|en|Japanese missions to Sui China|WD=Q15401774|lt=Mission to Sui|1=Japanese missions to Sui China}}) to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui|1=Sui dynasty}} in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Great King (Yamato court)|1=Okimi}}.</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|1=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Q29583251|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} * {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} * {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|1=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Q29583251|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} * {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} * {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多利思比孤]][[zh:多利思比孤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多利思比孤]][[zh:多利思比孤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多利思比孤]][[zh:多利思比孤]]
{{distinguish|Prince Ameoshitarashi}}
{{afc comment |imo only Yamatai figure with a shot of getting through afc but unlikely about it [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 07:42, 4 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox person | name = Tarashiko | birth_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kingdom of Wa | death_date = Unkown | death_place = Unkown | title = {{ill|Ōkimi|WD=Q718995|1=Okimi}} | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|1=Wakoku}} }}
{{ill|Tarishihiko|ja|多利思比孤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarishihiko|qq=}} was the king of Wa an ancient Japanese Kingdom.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deno |first=Fumiri |date=December 2022 |title=About the King of Han-Winu (漢委奴國王) on the gold seal |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/25138502221121682 |journal=Journal of Chinese Writing Systems |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=265–272 |doi=10.1177/25138502221121682 |issn=2513-8502}}</ref> He had the title of [[Okimi]] and his family name was Ame, so his name has been interpreted as Ame-Tarishi-Hiko.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Waida |first=Manabu |date=1976 |title=Sacred Kingship in Early Japan: A Historical Introduction |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1062152 |journal=History of Religions |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=319–342 |issn=0018-2710}}</ref> {{Special characters|description=Included in JIS X 0212 ([[Supplementary Kanji]])}} {{Wikisource|隋書倭国伝}} '''Tarishihiko''' (多利思北孤) was the king of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wa|1=Wakoku}} as recorded in the "Book of Sui" ({{lang|zh|隋書}}), specifically in the section "{{lang|zh|卷八十一 列傳第四十六 東夷 俀國}}". In the ''Book of Sui'', the term "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}" is used instead of the usual "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}" seen in other Chinese historical records.<ref>In Japan, "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|俀}}國" is usually regarded as a mistaken rendering of "{{Supplementary Kanji Font|倭}}國".</ref> The account also appears in the ''{{ill|History of the Northern Dynasties|en|History of the Northern Dynasties|WD=Q1188548|lt=Book of the Northern Dynasties|1=History of the Northern Dynasties}}''.
== Overview == It is recorded that Tarishihiko sent envoys ({{ill|Japanese missions to Sui China|en|Japanese missions to Sui China|WD=Q15401774|lt=Mission to Sui|1=Japanese missions to Sui China}}) to {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui|1=Sui dynasty}} in the 20th year of the ''Kaihuang'' era (600) and the 3rd year of the ''Daye'' era (607).
=== Name === The text states, "{{lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}" ("The king of Wa is surnamed Amabe, has the courtesy name Tarishihiko, and is titled Ahekime").<ref>See {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Great King (Yamato court)|1=Okimi}}.</ref>
=== Wife and Children === The text mentions, "{{lang|zh|王妻號雞彌 後宮有女六七百人 名太子爲利歌彌多弗利}}" ("The king's wife is named Kime. The harem includes 600–700 women, and the crown prince is called Rikameitarifuri").
=== Territory === The text describes the territory as follows: "{{lang|zh|夷人不知里數但計以日 其國境東西五月行南北三月行各至於海 其地勢東高西下都於邪靡堆 則魏志所謂邪馬臺者也}}" ("The people do not measure distance by ''li'' but by days. The kingdom's borders take five months to traverse east to west and three months north to south, with all directions leading to the sea. The capital, Yamidai, is the same as the Yamatai mentioned in the ''Records of Wei'' (''Weizhi'').")
The text also notes, "{{lang|zh|有阿蘇山 其石無故火起接天者俗以爲異因行祷祭}}" ("There is a mountain called Mount Aso, where rocks inexplicably ignite and connect with the heavens, leading locals to offer prayers and sacrifices").
Regarding the route of the Sui envoy Pei Shiqing, it states, "{{lang|zh|經都斯麻國迥在大海中 又東至一支國又至竹斯國又東至秦王國 其人同於華夏 以爲夷州疑不能明也 又經十餘國達於海岸 自竹斯國以東皆附庸於俀}}" ("The envoy traveled through Tsushima Island in the middle of the ocean, east to Iki, then Tsukushi, and then east to Qinwang. The people resembled those of Hua Xia, and their identity was unclear. Beyond this, the envoy traversed over ten countries to reach the coastline. East of Tsukushi, all were subordinate to Wa").
=== Governance === The text records, "{{lang|zh|使者言俀王以天爲兄 以日爲弟 天未明時出聽政 跏趺坐 日出便停理務 云委我弟 高祖曰 此太無義理 於是訓令改之}}" ("The envoy reported that the king of Wa regarded the heavens as his elder brother and the sun as his younger brother. Before dawn, the king would emerge, sit cross-legged, and listen to politics, but he would cease once the sun rose, leaving duties to his 'younger brother.' The Sui emperor Yang Jian disapproved, deeming it unreasonable, and ordered the practice to be changed").
Additionally, "{{lang|zh|内官有十二等 一曰大德 次小德 次大仁 次小仁 次大義 次小義 次大禮 次小禮 次大智 次小智 次大信 次小信 員無定數}}" describes a twelve-Rank system of officials ({{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}}).<ref>The order differs from that in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|en|Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|WD=Q3061873|lt=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu|1=Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu}}''.</ref>
=== "Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun" === In ''Daye'' 3 (607), the famous phrase "The Son of Heaven in the Land of the Rising Sun addresses the Son of Heaven in the Land of the Setting Sun" appeared in a diplomatic letter sent to Sui. This phrase, part of an envoy’s effort to study Buddhism, angered Emperor Yang of Sui ({{lang|zh|帝覧之不悦 謂鴻臚卿曰 蠻夷書有無禮者 勿復以聞}}). The term "Land of the Rising Sun" reflects expressions in contemporary Buddhist texts, such as the ''Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra''.
Though displeased, Emperor Yang regarded Wa as strategically significant due to its position behind Goguryeo and dispatched Pei Shiqing as an envoy. ''Nihon Shoki'' preserves the letter from Sui, starting with "The Emperor addresses the Emperor of Wa." Some scholars argue this reflects an alteration, but the inclusion of "Emperor" suggests mutual respect.<ref>Yoshimura Takehiko, ''Birth of the Ancient Emperor,'' Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 978-4047032972.</ref>
== Interpretation == The description in ''Sui Shu'' and ''Bei Shi'' identifies the king as male, conflicting with the female sovereigns of ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki''.
The identification of ''Tarishihiko'' remains debated, with some associating him with Prince Shōtoku, Soga no Umako, or others. The name "Tarishihiko" is often reinterpreted based on its linguistic components.<ref>Ishihara Michihiro, ed. ''New Translation of the Chinese Official Histories,'' Iwanami Shoten, ISBN 4-00-002403-7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Sokpo|ja|冊封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokpo|1=Q29583251|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29583251}} * {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} * {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} * {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} * {{ill|Prince Ameoshitarashi|ja|天足彦国押人命|1=Prince Ameoshitarashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ameoshitarashi|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~eh6k-ymgs/book/furuta/zuisho.htm ''Sui Shu Wa State Account'' (archived)] * [http://www.geocities.jp/intelljp/cn-history/zui/wa.htm ''Sui Shu'' Vol. 81, East Yi, Wa State (archived)]
